title: Custom Computer Programming Services

alternatives.name: Managed custom application development and maintenance firm
inclusion_criteria: Falls within the deterministic $1 million to $10 million EBITDA target band and is an independent operating business rather than a captive development function.

Earns its primary revenue by managing delivery of customer-specific enterprise applications, digital platforms, cloud applications, data systems, application modernization, product engineering, and continuing enhancement or maintenance.
Owns the customer relationship and delivery responsibility for defined software work rather than primarily supplying individual workers under customer direction.

Is not predominantly a public-sector mission contractor, web or creative agency, industrial or embedded programming specialist, dedicated-team labor provider, or proprietary-software hybrid as separately enumerated below.
exclusion_criteria: Software publishers and product-led businesses whose primary economics come from licenses or subscriptions to publisher-owned software.
Computer systems integration, managed infrastructure, hosting, data-processing, computer-facilities-management, help-desk, and general IT consulting firms for which custom programming is ancillary.
Businesses whose primary operating model satisfies another enumerated alternative in this specification.
alternatives.rationale: This is the central operating expression of the official industry definition and is repeatedly visible in primary acquisition announcements. Its count is an explicit allocation of the broad deterministic band, not an observed Census category; the range is wide because official data do not separate managed delivery from the other enumerated models.

alternatives.name: Public-sector mission custom-programming contractor
inclusion_criteria: Derives a majority of revenue from federal, state, or local government customers or from government prime contractors.
Performs customer-specific application development, modernization, testing, or support under public procurement and contracting structures.
Remains primarily a programming-services operator rather than a packaged-software vendor or hardware-led systems integrator.
exclusion_criteria: Commercially led managed custom-application firms with only incidental government work.
General professional-services, engineering, staffing, or systems-integration contractors for which custom programming is not the primary activity.
alternatives.rationale: Government-focused operators can satisfy the same official programming scope while having materially different procurement, pricing, customer-concentration, and sale dynamics. The base and range are unmeasured estimates pending a supplier-level reconciliation of official procurement and firm data.

alternatives.name: Dedicated development-team and staff-augmentation provider
inclusion_criteria: Provides customer-specific software developers or dedicated teams for extended engagements.
Revenue is primarily linked to supplied personnel, seats, hours, or team capacity.
The customer retains substantial day-to-day direction of the work or product roadmap.
exclusion_criteria: Managed delivery firms accountable for defined software outcomes, acceptance, and project execution.
Employment agencies whose primary activity belongs outside custom computer programming services.
alternatives.rationale: Long-running dedicated teams are a recognizable customer-specific programming model but are not separately tabulated within 541511. The broad uncertainty range acknowledges possible classification overlap with employment services and managed custom-development firms.

alternatives.name: Customer-specific web, mobile, and digital-experience development firm
inclusion_criteria: Primarily designs and develops customer-specific websites, portals, mobile applications, ecommerce experiences, or digital interfaces.
Programming and software delivery are primary rather than incidental to advertising, branding, media buying, or content production.
Falls in the target band rather than the much larger population of very small studios.
exclusion_criteria: Enterprise application and platform engineering firms whose work is not predominantly customer experience or web and mobile delivery.
Marketing and creative agencies for which programming is ancillary.
alternatives.rationale: The official 541511 index includes custom web page design, but no official target-band split isolates larger programming-led web and mobile firms from small studios or marketing agencies.

alternatives.name: Industrial, embedded, machine-vision, and robotics programming specialist
inclusion_criteria: Primarily develops customer-specific software for industrial robots, material-handling systems, machine vision, embedded equipment, or related operational technology.
Programming is the principal activity rather than hardware manufacture, installation, or engineering design.
Falls within the supplied target band.
exclusion_criteria: General enterprise application developers with only incidental industrial clients.
Engineering, equipment, electrical contracting, hardware, or systems-integration businesses for which programming is ancillary.
alternatives.rationale: The official 541511 index expressly includes several forms of industrial and robotics applications programming, but their representation in the target EBITDA band is not separately measured.

alternatives.name: Custom-programming and proprietary-solution hybrid
inclusion_criteria: Remains primarily classified as custom programming but combines customer-funded development with material proprietary accelerators, reusable platforms, licenses, or subscriptions.
Custom software services remain the largest activity, preventing classification as a software publisher.
Falls within the supplied target band.
exclusion_criteria: Pure or predominantly packaged-software, SaaS, or publisher-owned product businesses.
Custom-development firms whose reusable internal tools do not create a separately material revenue model.
alternatives.rationale: The official boundary excludes software publishers, but mixed services and proprietary-solution firms can remain classified by their primary activity. Their target-band count is not directly observed and is therefore assigned a small base with a wide range.

residual.name: Other, ambiguous, or misclassified 541511 target-band firms
residual.rationale: Residual covers uncommon customer-specific programming models, combinations that cannot be assigned by primary economics, and firms whose reported 541511 identity conflicts with observed activity. The base equals the deterministic band not allocated to the six explicit alternatives.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau 2022 NAICS definition and index for 541511
U.S. Census Bureau 2022 Statistics of U.S. Businesses annual tables

target_archetype.selection_basis: The six alternatives are a mutually exclusive primary-business-model enumeration of the supplied 5,767-firm target band. Their base allocations sum to 5,450 firms and the residual base is 317, yielding 94.5% enumerated coverage. The managed custom-application alternative has the largest base because it is the central expression of the official industry definition and is repeatedly documented in primary acquisition announcements. Official sources do not report these model shares, so every count is an explicit bounded estimate rather than an observed category. The selected range overlaps the public-sector and dedicated-team alternatives, making the identity of the largest archetype uncertain even though its base count is highest.

value_basis.scope: Employee and individual-contractor cash costs of managed custom application development and maintenance firms in the $1 million to $10 million EBITDA target band, expressed as a share of revenue and allocated across mutually exclusive operating roles.

labor_contractor_cash_cost_share.rationale: The selected archetype is a people-delivered software service in which engineering and delivery staff are expected to be the principal cash cost. A 68% base permits meaningful non-labor operating cost and operating profit, while the 50% to 82% range covers firms with materially different contractor use, delivery geography, utilization, pricing, and management overhead. This is a class-neutral bounded underwriting convention, not an observation, a broad-NAICS proxy, or a claim about technology exposure.
caveats: No target-archetype-specific employee-plus-contractor cash-cost dataset supports the base or range.

Employee compensation, payroll burden, benefits, and payments to individual delivery contractors should be included consistently; corporate subcontractors delivering separately purchased work should not be silently mixed into employee roles.
Revenue recognition and treatment of pass-through cloud, software, travel, and third-party implementation charges can materially change the apparent share.
Because this score-driving value basis is an unsupported assumption with NONE evidence quality, it forces the resulting record to remain UNPROVEN until replaced by target-specific evidence.

roles.title: Software engineering and technical architecture
roles.rationale: Assumed share for employees and individual contractors primarily responsible for application architecture, software design, coding, integration, database work, DevOps implementation, and senior technical leadership. Personnel are assigned here only when their principal operating function is direct technical creation or modification of customer-specific software.

roles.title: Quality assurance, testing, security, and release engineering
roles.rationale: Assumed share for personnel whose principal function is test planning and execution, quality assurance, application-security validation, build and release control, and delivery verification, excluding developers who merely perform these activities incidentally within engineering roles.

roles.title: Product analysis, business analysis, and experience design
roles.rationale: Assumed share for product, requirements, business-analysis, user-research, and UX or UI design personnel who translate customer needs into specifications and designs but do not primarily manage delivery or write production software.

roles.title: Project, program, and delivery management
roles.rationale: Assumed share for scrum, project, program, resource, and delivery managers accountable for scope, schedule, staffing, coordination, acceptance, and customer delivery, excluding account-development and corporate-management work.

roles.title: Sales, account management, partnerships, and marketing
roles.rationale: Assumed share for business development, sales engineering when primarily commercial, account expansion, partnerships, proposals, and marketing. Delivery managers serving an active engagement remain in project and delivery management rather than this category.

roles.title: Corporate leadership, finance, people, legal, and administration
roles.rationale: Assumed exhaustive residual for executive leadership and internal finance, human resources, recruiting, legal, facilities, and administrative personnel not assigned to a direct delivery, product-analysis, or commercial role. Owner compensation is allocated here or to an operating role according to the owner's principal function, never counted twice.

value_basis.rationale: This fixed fallback structure provides a complete class-neutral economic denominator when target-specific evidence is unavailable. It separates total employee-plus-individual-contractor cash cost from the mutually exclusive allocation of that cost across operating roles. The values are deliberately labeled ASSUMPTION/NONE, make no source claim, and do not reuse the broader NAICS 5415 occupational proxy.
caveats: The role weights sum to all employee and individual-contractor cash cost, not to revenue, headcount, hours, or the cost of services reported under any particular accounting policy.
Each person or contractor must be allocated once according to principal function; multidisciplinary work must not cause the same cash cost to appear in multiple roles.
The structure should be replaced, not blended, when target-specific payroll, contractor, quality-of-earnings, or cost-of-service evidence becomes available.
This unsupported assumption forces UNPROVEN readiness and must not be described as measured industry economics.

source_hints.source: U.S. Census Bureau, 2022 NAICS definition for 541511: https://www.census.gov/naics/?details=541511&input=541511&year=2022
source_hints.why: Defines customer-specific software writing, modification, testing, and support as the industry and distinguishes software publishing, infrastructure or hosting, and systems integration.

source_hints.source: U.S. Census Bureau, 2022 Statistics of U.S. Businesses annual tables: https://www.census.gov/data/tables/2022/econ/susb/2022-susb-annual.html
source_hints.why: Provides official firm, establishment, employment, payroll, and receipts tabulations by industry and enterprise size; it is the appropriate starting point for reconciling firms to the deterministic target band.

Primary target-company announcement describes an acquired custom-software firm delivering custom applications, digital platforms, enterprise data systems, product engineering, cloud application development, and continuing client delivery.

Primary buyer announcement documents acquisition of a custom-software development firm and describes the buyer as a technology consulting and custom-software-solutions organization operating a multi-firm acquisition strategy.

source_hints.source: Target-firm audited financial statements, quality-of-earnings reports, payroll extracts, or industry benchmarking restricted to managed custom-application development firms in the target band
source_hints.why: Needed to replace the broader four-digit occupational proxy with cost-weighted roles specific to the selected archetype.

source_hints.source: Census microdata or a reproducible business census classifying 541511 firms by primary revenue model, customer type, contract structure, and service mix
source_hints.why: Needed to quantify the target archetype against staffing-led providers, government-contract specialists, web agencies, industrial programming firms, and product-heavy hybrids inside the deterministic EBITDA band.

implementation: What firm-level evidence distinguishes governed production use across paid delivery workflows from individual experimentation or tool access?
What proportion of the selected archetype has production implementation across a material share of software delivery, testing, documentation, support, and internal operations?
What implementation time, integration expense, review burden, rework, security constraint, and recurring vendor cost are observed for representative firms?
What evidence supports the timing estimate for majority material use among competing firms rather than among individual developers?
commercial_retention: What share of target-archetype revenue is billed through time-and-materials, dedicated-team, fixed-fee, milestone, retainer, maintenance, or outcome-linked arrangements?
How do customer contracts treat reductions in delivery hours, changes in staffing, third-party tool charges, change orders, acceptance obligations, warranties, and renewal repricing?
Who owns reusable code, frameworks, accelerators, documentation, and other work product, and can the operator reuse them across customers without violating work-for-hire or confidentiality terms?
How do realized price, gross margin, project scope, and customer retention change for comparable engagements over an approximately 24-month period?
sale_availability: What share of the deterministic target band matches the selected archetype after separating staffing-led, government-contract, web-agency, industrial-programming, and product-heavy alternatives?
How many distinct strategic buyers and sponsor-backed platforms have completed acquisitions of matching U.S. firms within a stated recent period?
Are seller-supply or succession conditions directly documented for target-archetype owners, without inferring them from unsupported owner-age assumptions?
Do firm counts avoid treating establishments, branch locations, portfolio add-ons, and sponsor-backed platforms as separate sellers or buyers when they are not economically distinct?
valuation_robustness: What disclosed entry multiples apply to independent custom-application development firms with $1 million to $10 million EBITDA, excluding software publishers, SaaS businesses, and substantially larger public technology-services firms?
How do contract mix, recurring maintenance, customer concentration, vertical specialization, delivery geography, management depth, and owner dependence affect observed transaction terms?
What independently supportable exit multiple is appropriate for the same business model and size basis, without assuming a product-company or platform premium?
Are headline prices, earn-outs, rollover equity, seller financing, and acquired cash or debt normalized consistently before calculating comparable multiples?
operator_survival: At mature technology use, which customer needs still require a paid operator accountable for requirements discovery, architecture, integration, security, validation, deployment, maintenance, and delivery risk?
Which service lines are most exposed to customer insourcing, self-service platforms, software vendors, freelance marketplaces, or larger integrated technology-services providers?
Can the target remain an independent accountable operator if billed labor hours or project prices fall, and what repositioning or contract changes would be required?
Does any regulatory, security, contractual, or domain-responsibility mechanism preserve paid demand for the selected operator rather than merely preserving demand for software itself?

special_notes: This specification is class-neutral: it defines the industry and research boundaries without assigning automation attractiveness, implementation feasibility, capture, survival, valuation, score, readiness, action, or verdict.
The target-archetype choice is an explicit estimate of the central and plausibly largest acquirable business model, not an empirical coverage measurement.

Material alternatives inside 541511 include technical staffing or staff augmentation, public-sector and government-contract programming, small web-development agencies, industrial-controls or robotics programming, embedded engineering, and firms combining services with proprietary software products.

Commercial and public-sector customer mixes may create materially different contract economics even when both firms satisfy the same official NAICS definition; research must segment them if the difference is economically consequential.
The deterministic n_band is a broad industry estimate and must not be relabeled as the count of firms matching this archetype.
The broader NAICS 5415 occupational mix may be retained as an explicitly labeled proxy elsewhere, but it is not accepted here as a sourced target-specific cost-role basis.
Acquisition announcements establish that the business model is transactable; they do not establish seller abundance, target-band coverage, valuation multiples, or economic quality.
